author: rom1v

body:
ffmpeg was mentioned as runtime dependency. not knowing exactly what scrcpy uses out of ffmpeg i just packaged everything ffmpeg provides.
this is approach increases appimage build time. qt-faststart can be removed if it is not really required.
author: aditya24raj

body:
linuxdeploy can package required libs automatically. but some are not detected by it as they are loaded only at runtime. so we do iterative workflow. run the appimage see which missing file it is complaining about and package them in appimage. and repeat until no errors.

those extra files where selected because of that iterative workflow. first it compalined about libgobject, then libpango and then libpangoft.

libgobject was special case. error said something relating to invalid lookup g_uri_ref. turns out the appimage was loading libglib from appimage but libgobject from host. this mismatch was causing this error. so it was packaged with appimage
author: aditya24raj

body:
with AppImageKit, everything thing has to be done manually, as i remember. make directory structure, place executables and necessary libs.
with linuxdeploy we give it executable path with -e flag, and it packages executable, along with necessary libs(to some extent).
so for simple stuff we can do manually with AppImageKit but for anything else linuxdeploy provides better experience.
linuxdeploy is also mentioned on offical documentation for usage.
https://docs.appimage.org/packaging-guide/from-source/linuxdeploy-user-guide.html

linuxdeploy is also recommended for packaging native binaries.
https://docs.appimage.org/packaging-guide/from-source/native-binaries.html

Btw, isn't there an official debian/ubuntu package for these tools?
i don't think so. most of their tools are in .appimage format itself.

i''m open for suggestions to use any other packaging tool too.
author: aditya24raj

body: this PR will build appimage against latest ubuntu LTS. as github actions run on latest ubunut LTS(focal fossa, 20.04.4). it is not guaranteed to work on older than that.

author: yume-chan

body:
I think this part can be phased better, for example something like

```diff
- 模拟“双指缩放”：<kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+_按住并移动鼠标_。
+ 模拟“双指缩放”：<kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+_按下并拖动鼠标_。

- 更准确的说，在按住 <kbd>Ctrl</kbd> 时按下鼠标左键。直到松开鼠标左键，所有鼠标移动将以屏幕中心为原点，缩放或旋转内容 (如果应用支持)。
+ 准确的说，按住 <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>，然后按下鼠标左键。在松开鼠标左键前，移动鼠标会使屏幕内容相对于屏幕中心缩放或旋转 (如果应用支持)。

- 实际上，_scrcpy_ 会在关于屏幕中心对称的位置上用“虚拟手指”发出触摸事件。
+ 具体来说，_scrcpy_ 会在鼠标位置关于屏幕中心对称的位置上再生成一个“虚拟手指”。
```
author: yume-chan

body:
> I think this part can be phased better, for example something like
> 
> ```diff
> - 模拟“双指缩放”：<kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+_按住并移动鼠标_。
> + 模拟“双指缩放”：<kbd>Ctrl</kbd>+_按下并拖动鼠标_。
> 
> - 更准确的说，在按住 <kbd>Ctrl</kbd> 时按下鼠标左键。直到松开鼠标左键，所有鼠标移动将以屏幕中心为原点，缩放或旋转内容 (如果应用支持)。
> + 准确的说，按住 <kbd>Ctrl</kbd>，然后按下鼠标左键。在松开鼠标左键前，移动鼠标会使屏幕内容相对于屏幕中心缩放或旋转 (如果应用支持)。
> 
> - 实际上，_scrcpy_ 会在关于屏幕中心对称的位置上用“虚拟手指”发出触摸事件。
> + 具体来说，_scrcpy_ 会在鼠标位置关于屏幕中心对称的位置上再生成一个“虚拟手指”。
> ```

The last sentence will be easier to understand if changed into sth like:

`具体来说，在与鼠标位置关于屏幕中心对称的位置上，_scrcpy_ 会生成一个“虚拟手指”用于模拟点按操作。`

body:
```suggestion
Se la connessione cade casualmente, esegui il comando `scrcpy` per riconnetterti. Se il comando dice che non ci sono dispositivi/emulatori trovati, prova ad eseguire `adb connect DEVICE_IP:5555` di nuovo, e poi `scrcpy` come al solito. Se dice ancora che non ne ha trovato nessuno, prova ad eseguire `adb disconnect` e poi esegui di nuovo questi due comandi.
```
author: albertopasqualetto

# Seleziona l'unico dispositivo connesso via USB
scrcpy -d             # come adb -d
scrcpy --select-usb   # versione lunga

# Seleziona l'unico dispositivo connesso via TCP/IP
scrcpy -e             # come adb -e
scrcpy --select-tcpip # versione lunga
```
```

author: JohnVeness

body:
Unlike what would be expected, adb shell just concatenates the arguments as strings same
as if they were just separated by spaces. Sending the commands in one argument
or sending as multiple arguments doesn't preduce any different outcome.
Sad but that's how adb works.

try typing this in your local shell (you may need the `-s` argument for adb):
`adb shell 'am broadcast'`
vs
`adb shell am broadcast`

If they both work, means there's no reasonable way to deliver the arguments as-is, even when using `execvp` (which scrcpy is using).
Sadly, they have to be escaped.

Whether the escape be done here or before the calling stack to call adb shell is up for debate. However, no doubt it's needed and no doubt the behavior adb has is improper when intending to provide arguments as-is
author: brunoais

body:
> Unlike what would be expected, adb shell just concatenates the arguments as strings same
as if they were just separated by spaces

In fact, it depends on the adb version and the platform. It's a total mess. You can't rely on any specific behavior here. Adding quotes might break on some platforms and some adb versions.

Anyway, in practice, the command line should not exceed 256 chars (it [crashes](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/issues/2841#issuecomment-984425680) on some devices), so the script content could not be passed as a server argument.
author: rom1v

body: In that case, should I add sending the script to `control_msg.c` by queueing a message using `sc_controller_push_msg()` sometime in the beginning of the program execution while also implementing a new type at `sc_control_msg_serialize()` for that new feature (and then adding the counter-part code in the server)?
author: brunoais

body:
I don't know what to do. I have mixed feelings about this feature.

> should I add sending the script to `control_msg.c` by queueing a message using `sc_controller_push_msg()`

This would require to start the `CleanUp` process only after this message is received :/
author: rom1v

body:
> I don't know what to do. I have mixed feelings about this feature.

What about sending much of the first data through stdin? It can be encoded in some way and then just sent to stdin when `adb shell` is run.
With that, we can also deal with the issue of limited size of args, the argv inconsistency, etc... and just send all arguments through a stream in stdin as soon as the stdin stream exists. Maybe just encoding as base64 is enough and then ending with maybe the '\0'

> This would require to start the CleanUp process only after this message is received :/

Only if a script exists but yes, if a script was set, it would need to wait.
author: brunoais

body:
> What about sending much of the first data through stdin?

IIRC, `adb shell` does not forward stdin to the process it runs (contrary to ssh).
author: rom1v

body:
It works for me:
``` 
$ echo "love me" | adb shell 'cat -'
love me
love me
```
It is weird that `adb shell` first outputs the program it receives.
However, it is shown in the manual:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/639467/165170434-f96aa6e1-8667-4436-8d4c-190b56e1a431.png)
author: brunoais

body:
Indeed. Even with an old adb version (tested with 36).

However, the behavior is slightly different depending on the adb server on the device:
 - on my Nexus 5, the input is "echoed" to the output, and stdin is never closed (even if it is closed on the desktop side)
 - on my OnePlus 7 Pro, the input is not echoed, and stdin is correctly closed
author: rom1v

body:
> on my Nexus 5, the input is "echoed" to the output, and stdin is never closed (even if it is closed on the desktop side)

Which android version?

On my version:

1. the echo happens
2. stdin is not closed but it can be closed by the running program.
3. Terminating the running program correctly terminates `adb shell`

Do you think this can be tried?
author: brunoais

body: I have mixed feelings about this. IMO this feature is secondary, it adds complexity and requires to change how the parameters are passed, and apparently it does not behave the same way on all adb/devices. So TBH I'm not very keen it on for the moment :/
author: rom1v

body:
Alright. In that case this and the alternative way that was already shot down are shot down.
I'll see if I can find an alternative way to process this.

Maybe it can be sent as a `ControlMessage` message like most features are done. Possibly in a similar way that the clipboard message is sent.
However, it would be convenient to increase the limit to `1«19` 512KB. That way a script can be ~400KB which would allow a lot of flexibility.

I think that the downside of using the `ControlMessage` API is acceptable with that upside of stability between versions.

What do you think?
author: brunoais

body: @rom1v ?
author: brunoais

body:
Thank you for your work.

I guess you followed #98 discussions. After re-reading it, when I concluded:

> The video encoder does not work below 5.0.

Maybe it was related to the smart watch from the issue (without an hardware encoder) and not to Android < 5 after all. So indeed, it might be possible to support Android 4.4.

TBH, I'm not sure it's worth it to add support for 4.4 in 2022. It adds complexity and maintenance (btw there is still probably the `dalvik-cache` creation to handle). But if it works, maybe we could merge (anyway at least a separate up-to-date `api19` branch could be useful)… I have no Android 4.4 device to test unfortunately. I will try to find one.
author: rom1v

body:
Nope, the server should be buildable without any java/kotlin dependencies (and without gradle): [`build_without_gradle.sh`](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/blob/master/server/build_without_gradle.sh)

(Btw, I just removed the `implementation fileTree` line above: 7f2f5950f2624c1a3fd847af6248bcbef71a3124)
author: rom1v

body: I prefer not shadowing system libraries. I know that Android should give priority to the system ones if they exist (so this is used only if it does not exist on the system), but I prefer explicitly calling separate code if API < 21.
author: rom1v

body: Is it possible that the index is out of bounds? (or is it just an "assertion"?)
author: rom1v

body: I'm not sure this is necessary to add a wrapper, the impact is quite small in `ScreenEncoder` (basically just an if and a few lines), while the wrapper adds a lot of lines (and complexity) and an indirection at runtime for every call (probably negligible, but IMO the wrapper does not worth it in this specific case).
author: rom1v

body:
Hmm, is it OK to download a prebuilt into a directory (e.g. `prebuilt-compileOnly`) and also use everything in it as classpath for `javac`? That should work both ways. I really want the IDE to help me not call API 19 stuff on API 21.

> and without gradle

Any other reason than using build system within build system is weird? (And the flex of knowing how to manually produce an APK? 😁 )
author: consp1racy

body: Makes sense, let's do an `OsCompat`, as long as it's just one method and not the whole class it should be manageable. And it'll play nicely with the `@*Api` annotations. I'll either do the OO thing again or what they do in AndroidX Core. There has to be an indirection between the call to new API to avoid slow class verification.
author: consp1racy

body:
The beautiful thing about this is that `ScreenEncoder` basically changed by one line in exchange for API 19 support. I don't want to pollute `ScreenEncoder` with compat logic.

Actually, I plan to extract a `MediaCodecListCompat` as well to keep `ScreenEncoder` clean.
author: consp1racy

body: Come to think about it, this may be necessary only for "invalidated" buffers which had position/limit reset. The hidden API isn't even available on Kitkat. I'll remove this method entirely. Thanks!
author: consp1racy

body:
> is it OK to download a prebuilt into a directory

Nope. If it was a necessary dependency, we could discuss what to do, but here they are just annotations, it does not worth the dependency.

> Any other reason than using build system within build system is weird?

It is used to build the official Debian package: https://salsa.debian.org/yangfl-guest/scrcpy/-/blob/ab5d09e733bb9635b4410693ceff95dbc859dd4c/debian/rules#L41
There were problems with gradle/kotlin (Debian provides its own packages for android tools), and it is not acceptable for Debian to download dependencies while building (there must be a separate package built from sources…).
author: rom1v

body: I only want `@RequiresApi`, Android Studio will probably be happy if we just include this one class in the project. Then we don't need to manage a dependency.
author: consp1racy

body: I will have to put `libcore.io` package in a separate `compileOnly` module and make it work without gradle too. We don't want to ship that inside the package.
author: consp1racy

body:
For system-wide installations, Exec should use the system-wide `adb` installed at, e.g.: `/usr/bin/adb` and leave customization concerns up to the user in e.g.: `~/.local/applications/scrcpy.desktop`
:+1: good to merge

body:
`build_without_gradle.sh` would fail when being invoked from within a vanilla Ubuntu container. I think this is because `C_LANG` and friends were set to ASCII.  Since this repository uses UTF-8, it seemed to make sense to enforce that here as well, and this fixed the issue.
The PR as it currently stands no longer calls `build_without_gradle` directly so this is not needed for this MR.  I can undo the change if you want, but I don't think it's wrong ;-).
author: qmfrederik

body:
These images are generated and maintained by Circle CI, a provider of CI tooling & infrastructure.  I had a look at the container images which can be used for building Android apps, and this seemed to be one of the most maintained ones.  The source repo is here: https://github.com/CircleCI-Public/cimg-android.

That's a long way to say I think they are fairly trustworthy.  I tried to start from ubuntu:jammy and then install ADK from the Ubuntu package repositories, but the ADK that ships with Ubuntu is too old.  The other option is to start from a vanilla ubuntu:jammy image (or the vanilla image for your favorite distro) and manually install the ADK.  Or not run in a container at all, but directly on the GitHub actions VM, which ship with the ADK as well: https://github.com/actions/runner-images/blob/main/images/linux/Ubuntu2204-Readme.md#android.

In general, I think you're better of using containers because it shields you from any changes on the underlying build machines.

For the app, I would recommend running the build for the app in a separate job in a separate container, as the build dependencies are quite different: the server requires the ADK, the app itself requires headers for libav*, libusb, mingw and a bunch of other dependencies.

I originally had a job which would build the client as well, but then decided against it as I didn't have the time to fully complete that work (done is better than perfect, I guess).
author: qmfrederik

body: My dev container dockerfile is also based on cimg/android, just only contains the essential components for building Scrcpy: https://github.com/yume-chan/scrcpy-devcontainer/blob/9a7b20391574d25617241d07b75457c9f22dd1a2/.devcontainer/Dockerfile#L30-L51. The origin one needs much more disk space but it should not be an issue on CI.

Thank you for your contribution.

> Since SDL_CreateRenderer causes a fallback to OpenGL 2.1

Do you know where (in the SDL codebase)?

> if we request 3.3 and create a new context, we get the best OpenGL version it has to offer.

What if the latest version available is <3.3? It will fail in that case.

I'm a bit afraid that this change is specific to your machine/drivers, and that it may fail otherwise.

If OpenGL 3 or 4 can be available on macOS, IMO it should even be changed on SDL directly (we can still try to make it work better on scrcpy temporarily).

author: yume-chan

body:
Currently, the AOA/HID keyboard handles two different things:
 1. create the HID events
 2. send them over AOA

To reuse the HID part but using another protocol, your PR abstracts (2) (how to send them):
 - introduce a new trait `sc_hid_interface`, implemented by `sc_aoa` and `sc_uhid`
 - an instance is passed to construct a `sc_hid_keyboard`

This works, but it brings some complexity:
 - two levels of interfaces implementations (something implementing a `sc_key_processor`, with one implementation receiving something implementing a `sc_hid_interface`)
 - the need for an `async_message` flag (because not all implementations behave exactly the same)
 - all implementation have to `create()`/`destroy()` on the client-side (for UHID, I would prefer to create the UHID struct on the server side, the client should not have to handle all these details, like `vendor_id`/`product_id`, and the UHID handle should probably be closed on exit from the server side, without any message from the client)

I think "abstracting" the other way around ((1) extracting how to create the HID events) leads to a simpler solution (with less coupling).

My idea is to extract a component to create HID events (regardless of how they will be sent), so that separate keyboard implementations could reuse that same HID tools (without interfaces/traits) when they need to.

That way, we could get 3 separate keyboard/mouse "independant" implementations:
 - `sc_keyboard_sdk` (previously `sc_keyboard_inject")
 - `sc_keyboard_aoa` (previously `sc_hid_keyboard`)
 - `sc_keyboard_uhid`

Concretly:
 - `app/src/hid` would contain HID tools
 - `app/src/usb` would contain the AOA keyboard/mouse implementations
 - `app/src/uhid` would contain the UHID keyboard/mouse implementations

I implemented preliminary commits which perform all these refactors with the current features: [`uhid.10`](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/commits/uhid.10/)

From this branch, you could more easily add a UHID implementation (independently of the AOA keyboard).

What do you think?
author: rom1v

body:
This is a structure compiled for the computer architecture, it should not be used as is to pass it to the device architecture (in theory, endianness could be different for example).

IMO, the client should only send the descriptor, and the device construct the expected uhid structure on the server side.
author: rom1v

body:
> IMO, the client should only send the descriptor, and the device construct the expected uhid structure on the server side.

I'd like letting the client to specify device names (like `Scrcpy UHID mouse`/`Scrcpy UHID keyboard`), because the server doesn't know the device's type.

Client also needs to be able to specify `vendor` and `product`, because my gamepad rumble implementation only works with a specific pair of those (by emulating a Bluetooth Xbox One controller to load a special driver).

Because little-endian is so dominant on modern PC and Android, I don't know whether I need to deal with endianness even if those structures are created in Java. The header file is copied from Linux kernel so using C to handle them is the simplest way.
author: yume-chan

body:
> I'd like letting the client to specify device names (like Scrcpy UHID mouse/Scrcpy UHID keyboard), because the server doesn't know the device's type.

Sure. I'd pass all the necessary fields in a control message structure, with serialization/deserialization ((like the other control messages).
author: rom1v

body:
> I don't know whether I need to deal with endianness even if those structures are created in Java.

I guess so, you must pass a `byte[]` with the expected representation to your `UHidManager.write()` method.
You should probably use a `ByteBuffer` to build it (ref `ByteBuffer.order(…)`, `ByteOrder.nativeOrder()`).

To explain a bit more, I think it is incorrect that the client knows the UHID implementation details (including endianness, some fields that the client does not care about…). Moreover, it is possible to use scrcpy-server standalone (and control it from python for example). Even if we do not absolutely need to consider this usage, it still seems weird to expect to pass a Linux C-struct for UHID messages.
author: rom1v

body:
I implemented that on [uhid.15](https://github.com/Genymobile/scrcpy/commits/uhid.15/) (cf `UhidManager`):
 - the client sends UHID_CREATE and UHID_INPUT control messages containing HID data
 - the server (device) creates the binary UHID structures and write them to the file descriptor

It works well, but I haven't taken the commits for CAPSLOCK synchronization (HID output) yet.
author: rom1v

body:
Do you know why it reads 4380 bytes (with only the first byte being non-zero)?

EDIT: IIRC I read 2 events, with the first byte being 2 and 4 (UHID_OPEN and UHID_START). But why 4380 bytes?

old: That does not match `struct uhid_output_req` either ([documentation](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/torvalds/linux/master/Documentation/hid/uhid.rst)):
https://github.com/torvalds/linux/blob/603c04e27c3e9891ce7afa5cd6b496bfacff4206/include/uapi/linux/uhid.h#L81-L85
author: rom1v

body: Where did you find these values?
author: rom1v

body: 4380 is the size of `uhid_event`. It always reads/writes full `uhid_event`s.

 else
-    get_file "https://github.com/libusb/libusb/archive/refs/tags/v$VERSION.tar.gz" "$FILENAME" "$SHA256SUM"

body:
In theory, it would be better to check all video capabilities like you are doing there.

But in the past, I noticed that in practice (on some devices), the reported values were not always accurate (especially for sizes), where "valid" values don't work or "invalid" values works. That's why I don't use them to filter parameters.
author: rom1v

body: The program did not terminate abnormally due to a segmentation fault; instead, it exited with an 'aborted' log message.
author: kyoung-mo-yang

body: As you mentioned, if there are cases where the attribute values provided by the equipment are invalid, a comprehensive test of the entire equipment (within a specific range) will be required.

body: I'm assuming upstream ```dev``` had left it alone, but it's just a cosmetic change for those running ```scrcpy``` under ```zsh```. The file in question is just an auto-complete/help file associated with the program. I double-checked the file and there was no double mention of ```--rotate=```.
